mnemonics.title: LENGTHY INTEGERS
mnemonics.content: LENGTH() Yields Integer: Reminds that .length() returns int (not long/short). Essential for array index compatibility.

mnemonics.title: Zero-Based Reality Check
mnemonics.content: Length ≠ Last Index: 'Length is count, index is position'. For "Java" (length=4), last index=3. Prevents off-by-one errors.

mnemonics.title: No Property, Just Action
mnemonics.content: Parentheses Matter: Remember .length() is a method (needs ()), unlike arrays which use .length property. Think 'Strings work harder (need action)'.

mnemonics.title: Empty String Alarm
mnemonics.content: 0 = Ghost String: Visualize empty string "" as a transparent ghost. Check length before processing: if (str.length() == 0) { handle ghost }

acronyms.usage: Reminds strings are backed by char arrays. str.length() = array length (but array is hidden)
Key uses of string length: input validation, loop control, memory ops
Always check str != null before .length() to prevent crashes

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a string as numbered boxes: [J][a][v][a]. The count (4) is .length(), box numbers (0-3) are indexes
Null = black hole (no string), Empty = transparent box (exists but nothing inside). length() crashes on null, returns 0 on empty
Some characters like emojis 🚀 are 2 code units but count as 1 in .length(). Picture them as double-wide parking spaces
Strings are frozen in ice. .length() gives ice block size - can't change without making new block (new String)

analogies.analogy: Like a supermarket item counter - scans entire string instantly but O(n) time complexity is a myth (actual O(1) implementation)
Club bouncer checking ID length: if (password.length() >= 8) { allow entry } else { reject }
Train conductor counting cars: for (int i=0; i < train.length(); i++) { check each car (char) }
